text: The video displays the placement of five trocars for the laparoscopic procedure. The camera port is positioned above the umbilicus, while two operating trocars are located in the lower and upper right quadrants. Two additional trocars are placed in the left flanks near the pubic region to assist with rectal retraction.

The surgeon begins the operation with a peristomal incision to mobilize the terminal ileostomy, which is closed using a linear stapler. An Alexis retractor is placed at the previous ileostomy site to facilitate further creation of pneumoperitoneum. The apex of the pouch is marked on the abdominal skin before elongation. Inside the abdomen, uteropexy is performed for pelvic exposure, followed by identification and stitching of the rectal stump for traction and counter traction.

The surgeon performs a nerve-sparing rectal dissection, starting from the posterior plane, identifying the avascular plane between the pre-sacral fascia and the mesorectal fascia. The dissection progresses down to the pelvic floor, exposing the puborectalis muscle. The lateral and anterior mobilization follows, with the anterior dissection developed behind the rectovaginal septum along the rectal muscularis propria, aiming to avoid injury to autonomic pelvic nerves.

The anorectal junction is fully skeletonized, and the rectum is stapled using a double cartridge of a 45mm linear stapler approximately two centimeters above the dentine line. The surgeon then moves between the patient's legs to mobilize the small bowel mesentery up to its origin at the duodenum, demonstrating an elongation maneuver. The small bowel is exteriorized, showing a gain in length of three centimeters after intracorporeal ileal mobilization.

The surgeon performs a mobilization maneuver by removing mesenteric fat between the ileocolic vessels and the terminal branches of the superior mesenteric vessels, gaining an additional centimeter in length. A mesenteric serosal incision is made perpendicular to the mesenteric axis on both sides to further elongate the bowel.

The surgeon demonstrates a five-centimeter elongation at the apex of the pouch using various maneuvers. For further elongation, the terminal part of the superior mesenteric vessels is ligated after testing blood supply with a vascular clamp, gaining an additional three centimeters. An ileal J pouch is fashioned using multiple fires of a linear stapler. After reinduction of pneumoperitoneum, an ileal pouch anal anastomosis is performed with a twenty-nine millimeter circular stapler, followed by the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy and placement of a pelvic drain.
